content.content:
1	# Opposite-Colored Bishop Endgames
2	
3	> Endgames where each side has one bishop and the two bishops travel on squares of different colors, so they can never trade or directly oppose each other.
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1700–2400
6	**Prerequisites:** [Bishop vs Bishop Basics](bishop-endgames.md), [The Opposition](the-opposition.md)
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	When the bishops run on opposite colors, neither can attack or defend the squares the other controls. The board effectively splits into two non-overlapping worlds. In a pure pawn-up endgame this is the most drawish material in chess: the famous rule of thumb is that **even two extra pawns can fail to win** if the defender can erect a blockade on squares of his own bishop's color. The defending bishop sits in front of the passed pawns on its color, the attacking king and bishop simply cannot evict it, and a fortress arises.
11	
12	The reverse is true with queens, rooks, or many pieces on the board. In the **middlegame**, opposite bishops favor the *attacker*, because the attacking bishop dominates a color complex (often around the enemy king) that the defender's bishop can never contest. Same geometry, opposite verdict.
13	
14	## Why it matters
15	
16	Knowing the blockade saves and wins half-points constantly. As the *stronger* side you must learn the drawing tendency so you do not liquidate into a dead-drawn opposite-bishop ending a pawn or two up. As the *weaker* side you steer toward it deliberately: trading the right pieces into opposite bishops is one of the most reliable ways to rescue a lost position. And in the middlegame you flip the dial — keep heavy pieces on and aim your bishop at the squares your opponent's bishop cannot guard.
17	
18	## Diagrams
19	
20	```
21	8 . . . . . . . .
22	7 . . . . . . . .
23	6 . . k . . . . .
24	5 . p p . . . . .
25	4 . P P . . . . .
26	3 . . . . . . . .
27	2 . . . . . . . .
28	1 . K B . b . . .
29	  a b c d e f g h
30	```
31	*White is two pawns up but the dark-squared bishop blockades on c5/b6 dark squares — dead draw.*
32	**FEN:** 8/8/2k5/1pp5/1PP5/8/8/1KB1b3 w - - 0 1
33	Side to move: White.
34	[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/2k5/1pp5/1PP5/8/8/1KB1b3_w_-_-_0_1)
35	
36	The white pawns are fixed on light squares (b4, c4) but they cannot advance because c5 and b5 are covered. White's light bishop guards light squares; Black's bishop on e1 patrols the a5–e1 dark diagonal and stops any breakthrough. White can never make progress: a textbook **fortress**.
37	
38	```
39	8 . . . . . . . k
40	7 . . . . . . p p
41	6 . . . . . . . .
42	5 . . . . . . . .
43	4 . . . . . . . .
44	3 . . . . . B P P
45	2 . . . . . . . K
46	1 . . . . . . b .
47	  a b c d e f g h
48	```
49	*Middlegame logic in miniature — but here, pawns-only, this too is a draw; the win comes when heavy pieces stay on.*
50	**FEN:** 7k/6pp/8/8/8/5BPP/7K/6b1 w - - 0 1
51	Side to move: White.
52	[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/6pp/8/8/8/5BPP/7K/6b1_w_-_-_0_1)
53	
54	With only bishops, this is trivially drawn. Add a pair of rooks or queens and White's light-squared bishop, aiming at h7 and the light squares around the black king, would give a powerful attack that the dark-squared bishop cannot parry — the middlegame attacking principle.
55	
56	## Patterns and sub-types
57	
58	- **Two connected passers, both on the bishop's color:** if the defender blockades the front square, the pawns are frozen. Often a draw even two pawns up.
59	- **Two connected passers split by one file the defender's bishop controls:** the defender plants his bishop and king to stop one, blockades the other.
60	- **The "wrong" rook pawn idea extended:** a passed pawn whose queening square is the *opposite* color to the attacker's bishop can be a fortress with the lone defending king.
61	- **Distant passed pawns (two widely separated files):** these usually WIN, because the defender's single bishop and king cannot cover both wings — the key winning structure.
62	- **Middlegame with heavy pieces:** opposite bishops amplify an attack; the side attacking has an "extra piece" on the relevant color.
63	
64	## How to spot it
65	
66	```mermaid
67	flowchart TD
68	  A[Opposite-colored bishops] --> B{Heavy pieces still on}
69	  B -->|Yes| C[Attacker is favored aim bishop at king color]
70	  B -->|No, pawn ending| D{Passers connected or close}
71	  D -->|Connected| E[Likely draw blockade front square]
72	  D -->|Widely separated| F[Likely win two fronts]
73	  E --> G{Defender bishop controls block square}
74	  G -->|Yes| H[Fortress draw]
75	  G -->|No| I[Push and probe for win]
76	```
77	
78	## Worked examples
79	
80	```
81	8 . . . . . . . .
82	7 . . . . . . . .
83	6 . . . . . k . .
84	5 . . . . . P . .
85	4 . . . . . K . .
86	3 . . . . . . . .
87	2 . b . . . . . .
88	1 . . . B . . . .
89	  a b c d e f g h
90	```
91	*White to move; one pawn up — can White make progress?*
92	**FEN:** 8/8/5k2/5P2/5K2/8/1b6/3B4 w - - 0 1
93	Side to move: White.
94	[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/5k2/5P2/5K2/8/1b6/3B4_w_-_-_0_1)
95	
96	Solution: This is a draw. The pawn's queening square f8 is dark, but Black simply keeps his king and bishop controlling the pawn's path. After 1.Kg4 Bc1 2.f5+? is not even possible (f5 is occupied); White cannot shield the pawn's advance from the black king because his light bishop never guards f6/f8. Black just shuffles the bishop on the a7–g1 diagonal and keeps his king near f6/f7. The single passer cannot promote: **draw**.
97	
98	```
99	8 . . . . . . . .
100	7 . . . . . . . .
101	6 . . . k . . . .
102	5 . . . P . . P .
103	4 . . . K . . . .
104	3 . . . . . . . .
105	2 . . . b . . . .
106	1 . . B . . . . .
107	  a b c d e f g h
108	```
109	*White to move; two widely separated passers (d5 and g5).*
110	**FEN:** 8/8/3k4/3P2P1/3K4/8/3b4/2B5 w - - 0 1
111	Side to move: White.
112	[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/3k4/3P2P1/3K4/8/3b4/2B5_w_-_-_0_1)
113	
114	Solution: This wins. The pawns are far apart, so the lone black bishop and king cannot stop both. White advances the g-pawn (1.g6) and uses the bishop to support one passer while the king escorts the other. The defender must commit to one wing; the second front decides. The rule: **separated passers beat opposite bishops, connected ones draw.**
115	
116	## Common mistakes
117	
118	- Trading into opposite bishops when *up material* and then being unable to win — count whether your passers are connected (drawish) or separated (winning).
119	- As the defender, blockading on the *wrong* color square so the attacking bishop can chase your piece away.
120	- Forgetting that with heavy pieces on, the same opposite bishops mean *attack*, not draw — passively defending instead of seeking counterplay.
121	- Pushing a connected pair so they sit on squares the defending bishop covers, freezing your own winning chances.
122	- Letting the defending king reach the blockade square; the bishop plus king together build the fortress.
123	
124	## Train it
125	
126	- Lichess puzzle themes: **"endgame"** + **"bishopEndgame"**; filter for opposite-bishop motifs. Do 20 reps focusing on blockade vs. breakthrough.
127	- Drill the two-connected-pawns fortress (first diagram) against the engine from both sides, 10 reps each side, until the draw is automatic.
128	- Drill the separated-passers win (second worked example), 10 reps as the stronger side.
129	- Books: Dvoretsky's *Endgame Manual* (chapter on opposite-colored bishops) and Müller & Lamprecht's *Fundamental Chess Endings*. Read and replay every example once, then reconstruct three from memory.
130	
131	## Exercises
132	
133	1. **FEN:** 8/8/2k5/1pp5/1PP5/8/8/1KB1b3 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Can White win the two extra pawns home, or is it a draw? State the verdict and why.
134	2. **FEN:** 8/8/3k4/3P2P1/3K4/8/3b4/2B5 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Draw or win? Give the plan.
135	3. **FEN:** 8/8/5k2/5P2/5K2/8/1b6/3B4 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Is the single passer enough to win?
136	4. **FEN:** 7k/6pp/8/8/8/5BPP/7K/6b1 w - - 0 1 — White to move. Result with only bishops, and how would it change with a pair of rooks added?
137	
138	<details><summary>Show answers</summary>
139	
140	1. **Draw.** The pawns are fixed on light squares (b4, c4) and the dark-squared bishop blockades c5/b5; White's light bishop can never contest those squares, so the passers are frozen forever — fortress.
141	2. **Win.** The passers on d5 and g5 are widely separated. Advance with 1.g6 and use king plus bishop to escort one pawn while the other ties down the defender. A lone bishop and king cannot cover both wings.
142	3. **No, draw.** A single passer with opposite bishops cannot be forced through when the defending king and bishop control its path; White's light bishop never guards f6/f8.
143	4. **Draw** with bishops only. Add a pair of rooks (or queens) and White is much better/winning: the light-squared bishop targets h7 and the light squares around Black's king, an attack the dark bishop cannot parry — opposite bishops favor the attacker in the middlegame.
144	
145	</details>
146	
147	## Related
148	
149	- [Bishop vs Bishop Basics](bishop-endgames.md)
150	- [The Opposition](the-opposition.md)
151	- [Fortress](fortress.md)
152	- [Zugzwang](zugzwang.md)
153	
154	[[fortress]] [[blockade]] [[opposite-colored-bishops]] [[passed-pawns]]
155

content.text:
Let me verify each diagram, FEN, and analysis line systematically.

**Diagram 1 (lines 20-34):**
FEN: `8/8/2k5/1pp5/1PP5/8/8/1KB1b3 w - - 0 1`

Let me parse the FEN rank by rank:
- Rank 8: `8` → all empty ✓
- Rank 7: `8` → all empty ✓
- Rank 6: `2k5` → a6,b6 empty, c6=k, d6-h6 empty → `. . k . . . . .` ✓
- Rank 5: `1pp5` → a5 empty, b5=p, c5=p, d5-h5 empty → `. p p . . . . .` ✓
- Rank 4: `1PP5` → a4 empty, b4=P, c4=P, rest empty → `. P P . . . . .` ✓
- Rank 3: `8` → empty ✓
- Rank 2: `8` → empty ✓
- Rank 1: `1KB1b3` → a1 empty, b1=K, c1=B, d1 empty, e1=b, f1-h1 empty → `. K B . b . . .` ✓

ASCII matches FEN. Legal: one K each, one B each, no pawns on 1st/8th. Black not to move — is Black in check? White bishop on c1 (dark squares), black king c6. Not in check. Legal.

But wait — the text says "the dark-squared bishop blockades on c5/b6 dark squares". Let me check square colors. c5: c=3, 5 → 3+5=8 even → light square. Actually let me use standard: a1 is dark. A square is light if file+rank is odd... a1: file=1, rank=1, sum=2 even = dark. c5: file=3, rank=5, sum=8 even = dark. b6: file=2, rank=6, sum=8 even = dark. So c5 and b6 are dark squares. The black bishop on e1: file=5, rank=1, sum=6 even = dark. So Black has the dark-squared bishop. ✓

The text at line 36 says "Black's bishop on e1 patrols the a5–e1 dark diagonal". The diagonal from e1 going up-left: e1-d2-c3-b4-a5. b4 has a white pawn. So the bishop on e1 controls d2, c3, but b4 is occupied by white pawn. Let me think about whether this is actually a fortress/draw.

Let me verify this position carefully with a chess engine-style analysis.

content.text:
No engine available. I'll verify manually as a 2400+ player.

**Diagram 1 analysis:** Position `8/8/2k5/1pp5/1PP5/8/8/1KB1b3 w - - 0 1`. White pawns b4, c4 fixed (blocked by black pawns b5, c5). Black king c6, bishop e1 (dark). White king b1, bishop c1 (dark... wait).

Let me check White's bishop color. c1: file=3, rank=1, sum=4 even = dark square. So White's bishop on c1 is ALSO a dark-squared bishop! Both bishops are dark-squared. This is NOT an opposite-colored bishop position — it's a same-colored bishop position. This is a bug.

The text says "White's light bishop guards light squares" (line 36) and line 31 says "the dark-squared bishop blockades". But c1 is a dark square, so the white bishop is dark-squared, same as black's. This contradicts the premise of the article (opposite-colored bishops).

Let me confirm square colors precisely.

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
def color(sq):
    f = ord(sq[0]) - ord('a')  # 0..7
    r = int(sq[1]) - 1         # 0..7
    return "light" if (f + r) % 2 == 1 else "dark"

for sq in ["c1","e1","c5","b6","b5","b4","c4","f8","d1","d8","f1","g1","a7","h7","g6","h3","f3","b2","d2","c3","a5"]:
    print(sq, color(sq))
EOF

content.text:
Confirmed. **Diagram 1 bug:** Both bishops are on dark squares (c1 dark, e1 dark). This is a same-colored bishop position, not opposite-colored. The article is about opposite-colored bishops, so White's bishop must be light-squared. 

The text (line 36) calls White's bishop a "light bishop." So the intent is: White light-squared bishop, Black dark-squared bishop. The fix: move White's bishop to a light square. The bishop should be on d1 (light) to still be near and guard light squares, defending the structure. Let me also check b5 (light), c4 (light) — White's pawns are on b4 (dark) and c4 (light)... 

Wait, the text line 36 says "The white pawns are fixed on light squares (b4, c4)." But b4 is a DARK square. Another error. The pawns are on b4 (dark) and c4 (light) — mixed colors. And black pawns b5 (light), c5 (dark).

Let me reconsider the whole position to construct a correct fortress with genuinely opposite-colored bishops. The theme: White two pawns up, connected passers blockaded by the defender's bishop, dead draw.

Let me design a clean, correct standard example. A well-known fortress: White pawns on b5 and c5 (need black blockade) — let me think about the cleanest minimal construction with truly opposite bishops.

Standard setup for "two connected passers blockaded, opposite bishops draw":
- White connected passers, say c5 and d5. Black king blockades, black bishop covers the promotion approach on its color.
- The defending bishop must control the square in front of one pawn.

Let me build: White pawns c5, d5 (connected passers). White bishop light-squared. Black bishop dark-squared blockading. For the pawns to be blockaded, the black king sits on one blockade square and the bishop covers the other or controls the advance squares.

Actually, the cleanest classic fortress: pawns on c5 and d5, black king on d6 (blockades d-pawn front... no). Let me construct precisely.

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
def color(sq):
    f = ord(sq[0]) - ord('a')
    r = int(sq[1]) - 1
    return "L" if (f + r) % 2 == 1 else "D"
# Proposed: White Kb1, Bf1(light), pawns b5,c5? black Kc6, Bb4? 
# We want connected passers White on b4,c4 blockaded by black pawns... but then they aren't passers, they're blocked by pawns.
# Original had b4,c4 white vs b5,c5 black -> pawns BLOCKED head to head, not passed. That's fine: a locked structure.
# To be opposite bishops fortress: White light bishop, Black dark bishop. The structure b4/c4 vs b5/c5 is locked anyway; bishops irrelevant to win because pawns can't move.
# Square colors of the locked pawns:
for sq in ["b4","c4","b5","c5"]:
    print(sq, color(sq))
print("---blockade/key squares around")
for sq in ["a5","d5","a4","d4"]:
    print(sq, color(sq))
EOF

content.text:
Important realization: In Diagram 1, the pawns b4/c4 (White) vs b5/c5 (Black) are **locked head-to-head** — they are NOT passed pawns at all. They're blocked. So this is trivially a draw regardless of bishops; neither side can ever move a pawn. The bishops are irrelevant to the "fortress." 

This doesn't illustrate the article's theme (two extra passed pawns blockaded). The text describes it as "White is two pawns up" with "passers" being frozen by a blockade — but here White is NOT two pawns up (it's 2 vs 2, equal material) and the pawns aren't passers, they're mutually blocked.

So Diagram 1 has multiple problems:
1. Not opposite-colored bishops (both dark).
2. Not "two pawns up" — material is equal (2 pawns each).
3. Not a blockade of passers — pawns are locked head-to-head.

The exercise #1 (line 133, 140) repeats this same FEN and claims "two extra pawns" — also wrong.

I need to replace this with a correct minimal position illustrating the SAME theme: **White two pawns up, connected passers, blockaded by opposite-colored bishop = draw.**

Let me construct a correct, well-known fortress. Classic: White has connected passers c5+d5, Black blockades. The defender's bishop must be opposite color to White's, and Black needs no pawns (White is two pawns up — Black has zero pawns, White has two extra).

Construction: White Kc4, Bishop light-squared, pawns b5 and c5 (connected passers). Black Kc7 or b6 blockading, dark-squared bishop. Hmm, let me think about a genuinely drawn fortress.

Best known: pawns are on the same rank, e.g. White pawns f6 and g6, blockaded. Actually the cleanest is the textbook one. Let me construct and verify the draw logic.

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
def color(sq):
    f = ord(sq[0]) - ord('a'); r = int(sq[1]) - 1
    return "L" if (f + r) % 2 == 1 else "D"
# Theme: White two connected passed pawns, two pawns up (Black has none),
# opposite-colored bishops, draw because defender blockades on his bishop's color.
# Classic fortress: pawns b5 & c5 (connected). Black bishop controls the dark blockade square in front,
# Black king blocks the light one. White light bishop cannot evict the dark-square blockader.
# Front squares of pawns b5->b6, c5->c6:
for s in ["b5","c5","b6","c6","a6","d6"]:
    print(s, color(s))
EOF
input.description: Square colors for proposed connected-passer fortress

content.content:
b5 L
c5 D
b6 D
c6 L
a6 L
d6 D

content.text:
Pawns b5 (light) and c5 (dark). Front squares: b6 (dark), c6 (light). For an opposite-bishop fortress with White's light-squared bishop and Black's dark-squared bishop:

- Black's dark-squared bishop blockades on b6 (dark) — controls the b6 square so the b-pawn can't advance, and from b6 it also eyes c5/c7.
- Black's king sits on c6 (light) blockading the c-pawn.

White's light bishop can never attack b6 (dark square) to evict the bishop, and can never evict the king from c6 chase... actually the king can be attacked. Let me think about the classic drawn fortress more carefully. The well-known result: connected passers on the 5th/6th, blockaded by king + opposite bishop, is a draw if the pawns can't reach the 6th safely.

Let me instead use the canonical position from endgame theory. A cleaner, verifiable fortress: White pawns on **b6 and c6** would be too advanced (close to winning). The standard drawn fortress with opposite bishops and two connected pawns:

White: Kc5, Bishop, pawns b5+c5. Hmm. Let me just verify a concrete drawn fortress move-by-move. The simplest robust construction: pawns NOT yet on the 6th, defender's bishop controls both the dark advance square, king controls the light one, and the defending king/bishop can shuffle.

input.command:

# Build the position and reason about it.
# Proposal: 
# White: Kc4, Be4 (light), Pb5, Pc5   (two connected passers, Black has no pawns -> two pawns up)
# Black: Kc6, Bg1 (dark; on a7-g1 diagonal controlling b6,c5? c5 occupied) 
def color(sq):
    f = ord(sq[0]) - ord('a'); r = int(sq[1]) - 1
    return "L" if (f + r) % 2 == 1 else "D"
# Black king on c6 blockades c-pawn (c5->c6). To advance b5->b6 White needs b6 safe.
# b6 is dark. Black dark bishop controls b6 from a7 or c7 or g1-diagonal(g1,f2,e3,d4,c5*,b6,a7).
# c5 occupied by white pawn so diagonal blocked at c5; bishop on a7 controls b6 directly.
print("b6", color("b6"), "a7", color("a7"))
# If black king c6 and bishop a7: White Kc4, Pb5,Pc5, Bd5? d5 light, can't both. Use Be4.
# Can White ever play b6? b6 attacked by Ba7. If b6, Bxb6? no, after pawn to b6 the pawn is on b6 (dark), bishop a7 can take it: Bxb6. So b5-b6 loses a pawn or is met by Kxb6/Bxb6.
# Can White play c5-c6+? c6 occupied by black king -> illegal/blocked. King must be displaced first.
# White king tries to approach to support b6: but light bishop and king can't cover b6 (dark) which black bishop owns.
# This is the standard draw. Good.
# Check legality: Black king c6 adjacent to white pawn c5 and b5. Is black king in check from anything? 
# White Be4 (light) controls light diagonal e4-d5-c6! e4: e4-d5-c6-b7-a8. c6 is on it -> Be4 attacks c6! Black king in check. Illegal if White to move (black king attacked while it's white's move = black is in check on white's move = ILLEGAL, side not to move in check).
print("Be4 diagonal to c6?")
# e4 -> f5,g6,h7 (up-right); e4->d5,c6,b7,a8 (up-left). Yes c6 on it.
# So Be4 gives check to Kc6. Need a light bishop NOT checking c6. Put bishop on f1 or somewhere safe.
# Bf1: f1 light. diag f1-e2-d3-c4*(white king!) blocked. other diag f1-g2-h3. Fine, doesn't hit c6.
# But Bf1 must still be a real piece guarding light squares conceptually. Fine.
print("ok use Bf1")
EOF

content.text:
The connected-passer constructions risk being actually winning, which would defeat the purpose. Let me step back and use the genuinely safe, theory-backed fortress: the defender blockades with the bishop on the color of the pawns' advance squares, and the position is a known dead draw because the attacker's king can never invade. The most reliable is when the **king cannot approach** because the pawns and bishop wall it off.

The simplest bulletproof version is a position where both pawns are firmly blockaded and the white king has no entry. Let me verify the truly classic example: White pawns on b6 and c5 (or similar staggered) is risky. Instead, let me use the rock-solid blockade where Black's bishop alone holds both pawns by sitting on a long diagonal, and the king guards.

Let me reconsider — maybe keep it simplest and most defensible: pawns that are **not yet far advanced** (rank 4/5) with the black king and bishop forming a wall, white king cut off behind. Let me verify b4+c4 pawns (passers since Black has no pawns) with a proper opposite-bishop wall.

    f = ord(sq[0]) - ord('a'); r = int(sq[1]) - 1
    return "L" if (f + r) % 2 == 1 else "D"
# Pawns b4(D), c4(L) connected passers (Black has no pawns). White two pawns up.
# Advance squares: b5(L), c5(D).
# Opposite bishops: White light bishop; Black dark bishop.
# Black dark bishop controls c5(D) blockade square; Black king controls b5(L).
# Black king on b5? b5 light. attacked by white pawn? a4? none. c4 attacks b5,d5! c4->b5. So Kb5 in check from c4 pawn. bad.
# Black king on c5? c5 dark, blockades c... wait c5 is advance sq of c4. king c5 attacked by b4 pawn (b4->c5). bad.
# Same parity problem: each pawn guards the OTHER's advance square. King can't sit on either b5 or c5.
# So king must blockade from b6/c6 (one rank back) and bishop holds a 5th-rank square.
# Black Kc6 (light), controls b5,c5,d5,b6,c7,d6,d7,b7. Controls both b5 AND c5! So king alone covers both advance squares from c6.
#   Is Kc6 in check? white pawns b4,c4 attack a5,c5 and b5,d5 respectively — none reach c6. White light bishop must not check c6.
#   So Black Kc6 + dark bishop somewhere harmless. White can never push b4-b5 (Kxb5) or c4-c5 (Kxc5) because king covers both, and pawns don't defend each other's advance square enough?
#   b4-b5+: b5 attacked by c4? no, c4 defends b5? pawns defend diagonally FORWARD: c4 defends b5 and d5. YES c4 defends b5. So b4-b5, Kxb5?? illegal (defended by c4). Then b5 is a protected passer advancing!
#   Uh oh: c4 defends b5, b4 defends c5. So White CAN push: 1.b5 (defended by c4) and king can't take. Then pawns roll. This is likely WINNING, not a fortress.
print("connected passers where pawns mutually defend advances tend to WIN. Need bishop to actually stop them.")
# For a DRAW fortress, the defender's BISHOP must control a square the pawns must cross, and king the other,
# such that pushing loses a pawn (bishop captures an undefended advanced pawn).
# Working scheme: White pawns a-file rook pawn + b-file, where rook pawn's queening square is wrong color.
for s in ["a6","a7","a8","b6","b7","h8","h1"]:
    print(s, color(s))
EOF

content.text:
This confirms a deeper issue: **connected passed pawns on adjacent files with opposite-colored bishops generally WIN** (the famous result is that connected passers usually win; it's the *separated/blockadeable* configurations or when the defender's bishop controls a key square that draw). The article's premise in the prose (line 58: "Two connected passers... Often a draw even two pawns up") and the first diagram's claim are actually questionable as stated — but the well-established theoretical result is more nuanced.

Let me reconsider what the genuinely correct, drawable two-pawns-up fortress is, and pick one that's unambiguous. The classic dead-drawn fortresses with opposite bishops and two extra pawns are when **the two pawns are blockaded and the defending bishop controls the only breakthrough square** — typically the pawns are **doubled, or separated by a file the bishop controls, or one is a rook pawn of the wrong color**. The cleanest textbook draw: pawns on adjacent files but where the defender's bishop sits in front of one and the king in front of the other, AND advancing loses the pawn to the bishop.

For connected pawns where the bishop can stop them: the bishop must control the **rear** advance square. Let me build it carefully so that any push drops a pawn.

    f=ord(sq[0])-ord('a'); r=int(sq[1])-1
    return "L" if (f+r)%2==1 else "D"
# Known drawing fortress (Dvoretsky): the defending bishop controls the square in FRONT of the LAGGING pawn,
# king blockades the lead pawn; the pawns cannot both advance because one always lacks support and the bishop wins it.
# Concrete safe construction with pawns on the 5th, well-blockaded, king cut off:
# White: Kf5, Bg4(light), Pe5, Pf? no.
# Let's use widely-documented: pawns d5 & e5? advance d6(D), e6(L).
for s in ["d5","e5","d6","e6","d7","e7","f6","c6"]:
    print(s,color(s))
EOF

The cleanest is actually the position the article *prose* describes correctly: the defending bishop sits in front of the passed pawns on its color and the king helps. # FINAL robust construction:
# White: Ke4, Bc4 (light), Pawns d5, e5  (connected passers; Black has no pawns => White 2 pawns up)
# Black: Ke7, Bf6 (dark)  -- Bf6 controls e5? no e5 occupied; controls d6? no... f6 dark bishop diagonals:
#   f6-e5*(pawn) blocked one way; f6-g5-h4; f6-e7(king)? ; f6-g7-h8; f6-d8.  Hmm doesn't control d6.
# Need dark bishop to control d6 (the d-pawn advance, dark) AND/OR e... e-pawn advance is e6(light) -> king's job.
# Black King blockades e6 (light): Ke7 controls e6? yes e7->e6 adjacent. Also d6: e7 controls d6 too. King e7 covers d6,e6,f6,d7,f7,d8,e8,f8.
#   So king e7 covers BOTH advance squares d6 and e6! 
#   But d5 pawn defends e6 (d5->e6) and e5 pawn defends d6 (e5->d6). So if White pushes d5-d6, is d6 defended? by e5 yes -> Kxd6 illegal. push e5-e6, defended by d5 -> Kxe6 illegal. So pawns advance with protection again => likely winning. Same trap.
# CONCLUSION: classic connected passers DO win with opposite bishops when they can advance side-by-side protected. 
#   The DRAW requires the defender's bishop to control a square the pawns cannot pass — i.e. blockade where pushing is impossible because the square is bishop-controlled and NOT pawn-defended, OR the pawns are far enough back that king+bishop hold and white king is cut off and there is mutual zugzwang avoidance.

#   The famous draws are with ONE extra pawn, or pawns the bishop can blockade (e.g., separated by a file the bishop covers, or doubled, or rook-pawn wrong color).

content.text:
Now I have clarity. Let me build a **correct, verifiable drawn fortress** where the defending bishop genuinely stops two connected pawns because pushing would lose a pawn to the bishop (the bishop controls the advance square and the pawn there would be undefended). The trick: stagger the pawns so they're connected but one advance square is controlled by the bishop and NOT defended by the other pawn.

Let me construct: White pawns **b5 and c4** (connected, diagonal). Or use the classic where the bishop sits in front. Let me verify a position where the bishop blockades the front pawn and the king the other, push loses material.

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
def color(sq):
    f=ord(sq[0])-ord('a'); r=int(sq[1])-1
    return "L" if (f+r)%2==1 else "D"

# Construction where the BISHOP blockades a passer on its own color and that square's advance is NOT pawn-defended:
# White: Kc4, Bg2 (LIGHT), Pawns b5, c5? we showed b5,c5: advances b6(D),c6(L); c5 defends b6? c5->b6 yes; b5 defends c6? b5->c6 yes. mutually defended -> win risk.
#
# The reliable DRAW: pawns are blockaded one in FRONT by the bishop such that they can't move, and the white king
# can't dislodge the bishop (wrong color), and can't make a second threat. Use pawns where they are NOT mutually
# supporting because they're on the SAME file region is impossible for connected.
#
# Simplest sound DRAW to illustrate "opposite bishops are drawish, even a pawn up": ONE extra pawn fortress.
# But the section header (line 58) explicitly claims "two connected passers ... often a draw even two pawns up."
# That over-states theory. Safer to make the diagram a clearly-correct ONE-pawn-up draw OR a two-pawn draw that
# genuinely holds. A genuinely-holding two-pawn draw exists when the pawns are BLOCKADED and the defending KING+BISHOP
# wall off the white king with the pawns unable to advance because BOTH advance squares are covered by the BISHOP
# (king then free, no zugzwang matters since bishop has spare tempi on a long diagonal).
#
# Can ONE bishop control BOTH advance squares? They are opposite colors (b6 dark, c6 light for b5/c5) -> NO single bishop covers both colors.
# So the bishop covers ONE advance square (its color) and the KING the other. We showed king can cover both from c6 actually:
#  pawns b5,c5; Black Kc6 wait c6 attacked by b5 pawn (b5->c6) and by d-? only b5. So Kc6 illegal.
#  pawns b4,c4 (one rank back): advances b5(L),c5(D). Black Kc5? attacked by b4. Kb5? attacked by c4. 
#  Black king on c6 (covers b5 and c5 from c6): c6 attacked by white pawns b4/c4? b4 attacks a5,c5; c4 attacks b5,d5. Neither hits c6. LEGAL!
#  Black Kc6 covers b5,c5,d5,b6,d6,b7,c7,d7. So king alone stops b4-b5 (Kxb5? b5 defended by c4 -> illegal) hmm c4 defends b5.
#  b4-b5: defended by c4, so Kxb5 illegal -> pawn advances safely -> winning again.
# The mutual-defense of connected pawns is the killer. 
#
# THEREFORE: to make a real draw, BREAK the mutual defense: use pawns where advancing one is NOT defended by the other.
# Connected pawns b5 & c5: push c5-c6 -> c6 defended by b5 (b5->c6). push b5-b6 -> b6 defended by c5 (c5->b6). both defended -> win.
# Connected pawns b4 & c5 (not same rank, still connected/adjacent files): 
#   c5-c6: defended by b? b4 defends a5,c5 not c6. So c6 UNDEFENDED. If c6 is the dark bishop's color? c6 light -> dark bishop can't take. 
#   b4-b5: defended by? c-pawn on c5? c5 defends b6,d6 not b5. UNDEFENDED. b5 light.
# So neither pawn defends the other's next square here. Now a blockading setup can hold.
for s in ["b4","c5","b5","c6","b6","c7","d6"]:
    print(s,color(s))
EOF

This is getting into very intricate territory. Rather than over-engineer a borderline two-pawn fortress (which genuinely strains theory), the cleanest fix that is unambiguously SOUND and preserves the article's theme is to make Diagram 1 a **standard one-pawn-up drawn fortress with truly opposite-colored bishops** — and align the prose. But the section text (line 58) and the whole "even two pawns up" rule is a real, well-known endgame maxim — the issue is only that this *specific* position doesn't demonstrate it (equal material, same-colored bishops, locked pawns).

The most faithful minimal fix: build a correct **two-connected-passers blockade draw** that actually holds. The genuine textbook example (from Müller/Lamprecht) uses pawns where the bishop blockades the front pawn on its color and the king the other, with the white king unable to help. Let me verify the well-known holding position precisely.

    return "L" if (f+r)%2==1 else "D"
# Verify a real holding fortress, pawns b6 & c5 (connected, staggered), White 2 pawns up, opposite bishops.
# White: Kc4? let's place to NOT give check. Bf1(light). Pawns b6, c5.
# Black: Kc6? blockades. Bd8(dark) controls b6? d8-c7-b6 yes (if c7 empty). 
# squares:
for s in ["b6","c5","b7","c6","c7","a7","d6"]:
    print(s,color(s))
# Black bishop on dark controlling b6 (the lead pawn). King holds c6/blockade of c-pawn? c5->c6, c6 light.
# Black Kc6: attacked by b? no white pawn on a/b5; b6 pawn attacks a7,c7 not c6. c5 pawn attacks b6,d6 not c6. So Kc6 legal.
# But Black king on c6 and white pawn on b6: is b6 defended? by c5 (c5->b6) yes. and by? So b6 protected.
# Black to play could be ...Kxb6? c6->b6, b6 defended by c5 -> illegal. ...Bxb6? bishop takes b6, then cxb6 and pawn b6 vs Kc6... 
# This is the classic 'protected passed pawn b6 + c5' which is typically WINNING for White (connected, one protected).
print("b6+c5 protected connected => winning, not a fortress")

content.text:
The honest conclusion from theory: **two connected passed pawns on adjacent files with opposite-colored bishops generally WIN** (this is well established — e.g., connected passers on the 6th win trivially; even on the 5th they usually win because they support each other's advance). The genuine *drawing* fortresses with opposite bishops and extra pawns are when the pawns are **separated by enough files that the bishop blockades one and the king the other** and they can't combine, or one is a doubled/rook pawn.

So the article's section-1 maxim "two connected passers... often a draw even two pawns up" is **theoretically dubious**, and the diagram doesn't work. The cleanest sound fix that keeps the article's structure and intent: change Diagram 1 to the **canonical correct theme** — the *one-pawn-up* opposite-bishop draw, OR keep "two pawns up" but make them a configuration that genuinely draws (e.g., **doubled pawns**, which is the textbook "even two extra pawns don't win" case).

The most famous "two extra pawns don't win" opposite-bishop case is precisely **doubled or otherwise non-progressing pawns / a single file blocked**. Let me build the cleanest sound two-pawns-up draw: White has two extra pawns but they're **doubled** (e.g., c-pawns), trivially blockaded by the bishop on its color. That perfectly and correctly illustrates "even two extra pawns can fail to win."

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
def color(sq):
    f=ord(sq[0])-ord('a'); r=int(sq[1])-1
    return "L" if (f+r)%2==1 else "D"
# Better: keep the article's "two connected passers blockaded" theme but make it the well-known DRAWN case where
# the defender's bishop controls the square in front and the white king is shouldered out.
# The cleanest TRUE fortress (from FCE, Müller&Lamprecht) with two connected pawns that does NOT win is when the
# pawns are still far back (e.g., 4th rank) and the black king blockades in front while the bishop guards, and the
# white king cannot make a SECOND weakness. But we saw connected pawns self-support an advance...
# 
# Resolve cleanly with SEPARATED-by-one-file? No, article's distant-passers WIN.
# Cleanest indisputably-correct two-pawn DRAW: pawns on b5 and c5 are connected and CAN win; so instead use the
# rook-pawn-fortress that the article itself lists (line 60): a passed pawn whose queening square is opposite color
# to the attacker's bishop = fortress. Two pawns up via an a-pawn (wrong-color rook pawn) + an extra blockaded pawn.
#
# SIMPLEST SOUND: the most standard 'opposite bishops, extra pawns, draw' teaching position:
#   White Kf5, Bh5, pawns g5+h5? no bishop+pawn same square.
# Let me just do the WRONG-COLOR ROOK PAWN + one more, a classic dead draw:
# White: Kf6, Bc2(light), Pawns g6, h5? 
for s in ["g6","h5","h6","h7","h8","g7","g8","f7","f8"]:
    print(s,color(s))
EOF

content.text:
Let me make a decision and build the final position carefully. I'll keep the article's stated theme — "two connected passers, blockaded, draw even two pawns up" — but build a position that genuinely holds. The honest, verifiable holding mechanism: the **two connected pawns are blockaded on the 5th/6th by the king on the light square in front and the bishop on the dark square in front, and the white king is permanently cut off so it can never support a breakthrough; critically the pawns can't both advance because each advance square that ISN'T pawn-defended is bishop-controlled.**

The genuinely robust case where this holds is the **wrong-rook-pawn-style** corner fortress applied to connected pawns: when the pawns are **g6+h6** type and the king is boxed in the corner of the *wrong* color for White's bishop. Let me verify the well-known g6/h6 (or analogous) draw with opposite bishops.

Actually, the single most famous, indisputable "two extra connected pawns = draw, opposite bishops" position is from **Averbakh**: the defending king sits in front of the pawns, bishop guards, and the attacker's king is shouldered out. Let me verify the canonical one move-by-move.

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
def color(sq):
    f=ord(sq[0])-ord('a'); r=int(sq[1])-1
    return "L" if (f+r)%2==1 else "D"
# Canonical Averbakh fortress: White g+h pawns on the 6th vs lone bishop+king, opposite bishops, DRAW
# when the defending king reaches the corner in front and the bishop controls the queening diagonal.
# Position: White Kf6, Pg6, Ph6, B light (controls light promotion h7? h8 dark, g8 light).
#   Wait h-pawn queens on h8(dark); g-pawn queens g8(light). For a DRAW the defender holds.
# The classic DRAW: pawns g6+h6, White king f6, Black king g8/h8, Black DARK bishop controls h8 corner? 
#   h8 is DARK. If White's bishop is LIGHT it can't cover h8. Black DARK bishop sitting controlling g7/h8.
# Black: Kg8, Be5(dark) controlling h8 and the a1-h8 diagonal (e5-f6*? f6 is white king, blocks). Use Bd4: d4-e5-f6*(king) blocked. 
#   Use Black bishop on the OTHER dark diagonal to h8: g7-h8 or f6-... The square h8 is reached on a1-h8 diag (a1..h8) or only.
#   Bishop controlling h8 from b2,c3,d4,e5,f6,g7. f6 occupied by WK. So bishop on g7 (controls h8, f6-blocked behind WK). g7 dark.
# Let's set: White Kf6, Bb1(light), Pg6, Ph6. Black Kg8, Bg7(dark).
# Legality: Black king g8. Attacked? White Ph6 attacks g7 (h6->g7) -> attacks Black BISHOP on g7 not king. Pg6 attacks f7,h7 not g8. WK f6 adjacent to g7,f7,g6.. not g8 (f6 and g8 not adjacent). Bb1 light diag b1-..-h7? b1-c2-d3-e4-f5-g6*(pawn) blocked. ok. So Kg8 not in check. White to move legal.
#   But Black Bg7: is it attacked by Ph6 (h6xg7+)? yes h6 pawn can capture g7. That means White can play hxg7 -- then Kxg7, and White has Kf6,Bb1,Pg6 vs Kg7: g-pawn on g6, king g7 in front, g6 queens on g8(light) which White's LIGHT bishop controls -> that's actually winning side? 
#   This is getting messy. The real Averbakh draw has the bishop NOT capturable and the king cornered.
print("squares:")
for s in ["g6","h6","g7","h7","g8","h8","f6","f7","f8"]:
    print(s, color(s))
EOF
